explanation:
The property specifies that a 'controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi' (a control-traffic message is sent from the Local Node of the OLSR to DM_i) must be sent only after the Local Node has queried both the MPR Selector Set and Duplicate Set components, and this is required for every DM_i. In LTL, we capture this by stating that globally, whenever a controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi occurs, it must be preceded (at some point in the past) by both querying the MPR Selector Set and by querying the Duplicate Set. However, classic LTL describes future-oriented properties. To express that a certain event should happen only after other events, we can use the 'until' operator so that before sending the control-traffic message, neither sending happens unless both queries have occurred. Here, we require that controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi does not occur until after local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set and local_node_queried_duplicate_set have occurred (i.e., both have happened). As boolean combinations in 'until' are tricky in LTL, the usual idiom is: globally, if the control-traffic message is sent, then previously both queries have happened. Translating this, we get: G((controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) -> (F[0:1](local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set))). But since F[0:1](p) is just (p | Xp), and we want to make sure both have happened at some previous time, we use that whenever the control-traffic message is sent, the queries must occur before (necessarily in some order, but both must precede the message). Given LTL's limitations with explicit past operators, the more common idiom is to block the message until both queries have happened, e.g. (!controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) U (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set), then after that, sending is allowed any time.

Thus, to ensure the message is sent only after both queries, we use:
G( ( !controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi ) U ( local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set ) | X(!controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) )

Alternative but less idiomatic forms or omitting both queries would fail to require both queries explicitly. This translation best captures the intent given the available atomic propositions and LTL semantics.

The property specifies that 'the control-traffic message must be sent only after querying the MPR Selector Set and Duplicate Set components.' Using the atomic propositions available, the relevant ones are: controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi, local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set, and local_node_queried_duplicate_set. 
In LTL, this is best captured by stating that globally, whenever the controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi occurs, it must be preceded (somewhere in the past) by both local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set and local_node_queried_duplicate_set. Since standard LTL does not have past operators and we can only use future operators, we instead enforce that the message is not sent until both queries have occurred.
So, globally, it must always be the case that controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi does not happen until both local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set and local_node_queried_duplicate_set have occurred. That's expressed as:
G ( !controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi U (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set) )
This means: globally, controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi cannot happen before both the MPR Selector Set and Duplicate Set are queried, thus capturing the original English requirement.

The natural language states that a control-traffic message (represented by the atomic proposition controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) must be sent only after querying both the MPR Selector Set (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set) and Duplicate Set (local_node_queried_duplicate_set), and that this property must hold for every DM_i. LTL can express the requirement 'must be sent only after', which can be encoded as: globally, whenever a control-traffic message is sent, it must have been preceded (possibly immediately) by queries to the MPR Selector Set and Duplicate Set. In LTL, the correct way to enforce this causality (without referring to numbers or explicit time distances) is: globally, if a controltraffic message is sent, then previously (in the past, but vanilla LTL does not have past operators), or equivalently, globally, if a controltraffic message is sent, then at some time before (or at the same time) both queries have occurred, and since there are no past operators, the usual idiom is: globally, it is always the case that before sending a controltraffic message, neither of the queries has not yet occurred (which is non-trivial in pure future LTL). The most direct future-LTL encoding uses the 'until' operator to say: it is always the case that if neither query has occurred, then controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi can't happen until both queries have happened. So we write: G( (!local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set | !local_node_queried_duplicate_set) -> (!controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi U (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set)) ). This ensures that control-traffic is never sent before both queries have occurred. Earlier, less accurate attempts might not require both queries or might not strictly enforce the order, or might allow the message to be sent before queries, but this formulation precisely captures both the necessity and the ordering constraint.

The natural language states that the control-traffic message should only be sent after querying both the MPR Selector Set and Duplicate Set components, and that this must hold for every DM_i (which implies at every point, globally). The correct LTL translation should enforce that whenever a control-traffic message is sent, it must be preceded (in the past) by queries to both the MPR Selector Set and the Duplicate Set. Since LTL is future-oriented, we need to capture this causality as: globally, if the controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi occurs, then previously local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set and local_node_queried_duplicate_set must have occurred. In forward-looking LTL, this can be expressed as: globally, 'if controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi happens, then in the past local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set and local_node_queried_duplicate_set have both happened'. Since LTL does not support past operators, a standard way to capture this is: It is always the case that we do not send the control-traffic message before both queries have occurred, or more formally, until both have happened, we cannot send. Hence, the controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi is only true after both queries have occurred at least once. This is captured by '(!controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) U (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set)' and then after that point, controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi can occur. To enforce this every time the message is sent, a more precise form is: globally, whenever controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi, both local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set and local_node_queried_duplicate_set occurred earlier (since we lack past operators, the typical approach is to require that the queries happen right before the send action, or, as in this translation, that the send does not happen before both queries).

The input natural language specifies that the control-traffic message (represented by the atomic proposition 'controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi') must be sent only after querying the MPR Selector Set and Duplicate Set components (represented by 'local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set' and 'local_node_queried_duplicate_set'). This means that every time a control-traffic message is sent, both queries must have occurred before or at the same time. In LTL, this is commonly captured by requiring that, globally, whenever the control-traffic message is sent, the two queries must have already happened earlier (or at the same moment). This is translated by: G (controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi -> (F[0:1] local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & F[0:1] local_node_queried_duplicate_set)). This states that whenever the control-traffic message is sent, both queries must have just happened at the same time, which may not be strict enough. To strictly enforce 'only after', we use 'until' to capture that the sending cannot happen before both queries have occurred: ((¬controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) U (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set)) W controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi, but in standard LTL, enforcing 'only after' for every occurrence is best captured by G(controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi -> (past local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & past local_node_queried_duplicate_set)), but since LTL does not support past, the common forward-looking approximation is: control-traffic message implies that in the past the queries were made, which is approximated as: 'it is never the case that control-traffic message is sent before both queries', which in forward LTL is: (!(controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) U (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set)) W controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi. However, with the given atomic propositions, the most standard way is: G(((local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set) U controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) | G(!controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi)), which states that the message is never sent before both queries, for every occurrence.

Previous weaker formulations that do not use 'until' may allow the message to be sent without enforcing that *both* queries strictly precede it, so 'U' is preferred for capturing 'only after'.

The natural language description specifies that a control-traffic message (represented by 'controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi') must be sent only after querying both the MPR Selector Set ('local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set') and the Duplicate Set ('local_node_queried_duplicate_set'). To capture this in LTL, we want to ensure that every time a control-traffic message is sent, it is preceded (somewhere in the past) by queries to both components, and that there is no control-traffic message sent before both queries occur. In LTL, this can be encoded by stating that globally, it is never the case that a control-traffic message is sent and at least one of the queries hasn't happened yet. This is captured by: G((!controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) | (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set)). This formula ensures that it is always the case that a control-traffic message is only sent after (or at least, never before) both queries to the required sets have occurred. Previous LTL formulas might not correctly represent both queries as required, or might allow sending the message after only one or ignore the ordering, which this formula directly addresses.

The property describes that a control-traffic message should be sent only after querying both the MPR Selector Set and the Duplicate Set. This suggests that, globally, whenever 'controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi' occurs, it must be the case that before this event, both 'local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set' and 'local_node_queried_duplicate_set' have occurred. In LTL, this can be captured by stating that globally, if the control-traffic message is sent, then in the past (which in standard LTL is modeled using future operators), it must be that both the MPR Selector Set and Duplicate Set were queried previously. Since we only have future LTL in standard syntax and backward-in-time is not available, we usually encode it as: The control-traffic message cannot be sent until both queries have occurred. Thus, the property is: globally, it is always the case that if the control-traffic message is sent, then previously both queries have occurred; equivalently, the control-traffic message cannot occur before both queries. This is expressed as ("not (controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi)") until ("local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set" and "local_node_queried_duplicate_set").

Previous LTLs that do not enforce both queries before sending the message, or that only enforce one of the queries, or that allow the control-traffic message to be sent before both queries, would not capture this requirement correctly. The released-based encoding can also be used, but this until encoding directly captures the constraint on ordering.

The property requires that a control-traffic message ('controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi') must only be sent after the Local Node has queried both the MPR Selector Set ('local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set') and the Duplicate Set ('local_node_queried_duplicate_set'). This condition must hold globally, for each DM_i (the property is specified in terms of events; even though 'for each DM_i' is mentioned, atomic propositions implicitly capture an arbitrary DM_i). The appropriate LTL expression is: globally, if a control-traffic message is sent, then at some earlier point both queries must have happened (which is captured by requiring that globally, if the message is sent, then both queries have happened in the past, i.e., with the 'since' relation. Since plain LTL lacks past operators, the property is conservatively captured by ensuring that globally, the message can only be sent if BOTH queries have happened IN THE PAST OR AT THE CURRENT TIME -- in practice, the common LTL idiom is to require that the queries happen before the message is sent). This can be enforced by 'it is always the case that the message is not sent until both queries have occurred'. This is expressed using U (until): G ((!(controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi)) U (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set))

This ensures that the message will not be sent before the two queries are both true. This is conservative and directly captures the intent in the absence of past operators.

The property states that a control-traffic message (atomic proposition: controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) must be sent only after querying both the MPR Selector Set (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set) and the Duplicate Set (local_node_queried_duplicate_set). Since 'must be sent only after' implies that the sending of the message cannot occur until after both queries have taken place, we express this as: it is always the case that if the control-traffic message is sent, then previously (in the past) both queries have happened. In LTL, since we do not have past operators, we use the 'until' (U) operator to enforce that sending cannot happen before both queries. The LTL formula expresses that 'not (controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi)' holds until (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set) become true, after which the control-traffic message may be sent. This ensures that both queries always precede message sending. (If this property were previously written as simply controlling the order with implication, it might not preclude the message from being sent before the queries; the use of 'U' here guarantees the correct order.)

The property states that a control-traffic message should only be sent after querying both the MPR Selector Set and the Duplicate Set. To capture this in LTL, we want to ensure that globally (for every moment in time), if a controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi occurs, then there must have been a local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set AND a local_node_queried_duplicate_set beforehand (i.e., somewhere in the past). In pure LTL, this is captured by requiring that globally, the sending of the control-traffic message is only permitted if both queries happened previously, or, equivalently, that it is not possible to send the message unless both queries have already occurred. The formula 'G (controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi -> (P local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & P local_node_queried_duplicate_set))' would be correct with past operators. However, standard LTL does not provide past operators, so we must instead require that controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi cannot occur before both queries have happened. This can be captured using the 'until' operator: '¬controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi' must be true until both queries have occurred. Once both have occurred, the message can be sent at any time. Thus, using LTL, this becomes: (¬controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) U (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set). Note that applying this 'globally' ensures it holds for every execution. If other previously given formulas simply say something like 'queries eventually occur before message' without the until restriction, they may not properly forbid sending controltraffic messages before both queries; thus, the 'until' construction is both necessary and sufficient.
